item.title: The Democratic Trust Deficit
item.description: It’s like they never left. The Bidens are back in the news. Jill Biden has a book out this week where she tries to set the record straight about the debate, President Biden’s health, and his decision to run again. In an interview with CBS News, Jill Biden said the following about her reaction to…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
It’s like they never left.

The Bidens are back in the news. Jill Biden has a book out this week where she tries to set the record straight about the debate, President Biden’s health, and his decision to run again.

In an interview with CBS News, Jill Biden said the following about her reaction to the debate.

I don’t know what happened. As I watched it, I thought, ‘Oh, my God, he’s having a stroke.’ And it scared me to death.

Biden went on to say that she had never seen him like that before or since, and in the book, she writes about how it was possible that the president may have accidentally taken codeine cough syrup or Ambien.

These comments infuriated many, including a lot of former Biden staffers, because they were such a stark contrast to what she said publicly after the debate and the way the Biden high command reacted to anyone who called out Joe Biden’s abysmal and alarming performance in that debate. As someone who was pretty vocal about Biden dropping out after the debate and caught a lot of slings and arrows from the Biden White House, it was stunning to see Jill Biden essentially admit that she and everyone else had been gaslighting us for years.

The whole thing has reignited a big intra-party fight of 2024 between people who blame Biden for Trump’s return and those who blame the people who wanted Biden to drop out.

Like many of you, I am pretty tired of that debate (perhaps that’s because my social media mentions are still filled with people very, very mad at me for thinking Biden should drop out of the race). That conversation is relevant for the history books, and at some point, I would love to see some of the people involved take even a modicum of responsibility for their actions.

But Jill Biden’s reemergence and her admission that she was lying to all of us in 2024 is emblematic of a much larger problem that still plagues the Democratic Party with implications for 2026, 2028 and beyond.

The broken trust that comes from the President and most of the party telling the public not to believe their eyes when it comes to Biden’s age, and that they were silly to think that an 82-year-old shouldn’t run for reelection.

The party’s approach to Biden’s age was part of a broader pattern. They told voters that inflation wasn’t real, that a genocide wasn’t happening in Gaza, and that they had a plan to win when they never did.

Working to rebuild that trust needs to be at the center of the Democratic Party’s strategy.

Much digital ink has been spilled in this newsletter and elsewhere about the Democratic Party’s brand problem. For much of the 18 months since the 2024 election, Democratic Party approval has been at or near an all-time low.

To use a technical term, the party’s approval has been in the toilet.

Republicans hate the Democratic Party at about the same rate they always have, so the factors driving the party’s sinking rankings are Democrats not liking their own party and Independents souring on Democrats.

Among Democrats, there is a significant dissatisfaction with the party leadership. This shows up in polls. In a 2025 Reuters poll, nearly two-thirds of Democrats want their leaders replaced.

Those numbers have probably improved somewhat since then, as Democrats have performed well in the 2025 elections and notched some wins on Capitol Hill. However, it’s clear that broad dissatisfaction with the party remains. Senator Schumer’s numbers among Democrats are pretty bad, and in the most recent Quinnipiac poll, 93 percent of Democrats believe Congressional Democrats should be doing more to stand up to President Trump.

Among Independents, concerns about Biden come up more directly. Pollster G. Elliott Morris asked voters about their feelings about the two parties. Here’s what he found from Independents:

Independents land somewhere different. Their complaints cluster around general dissatisfaction and messaging — the sense that they don’t know what Democrats stand for or what the party is actually trying to do. Two specific themes show up that don’t appear in the Democratic column: The first is a lingering Biden grievance: independents in our open-end repeatedly cite the party “lying about Biden’s health,” keeping his campaign going “far too long,” and “anointing” Kamala Harris as the nominee without a competitive process. The second is a leadership vacuum complaint — variations on “lack of leadership” and “I don’t know what they stand for.”

Ultimately, I feel sort of silly digging around to find data to prove the point that the way the party handled Biden’s age, his decision to run for reelection, and the aftermath of the debate did real damage. Long before the meltdown on stage, 80 percent of Americans and a majority of Democrats told pollsters they thought Biden was too old to run for reelection, yet he decided to do so anyway with the backing of the entire party. Even after the debate, most of the party publicly stuck with the President and absurdly echoed the idea that this was one bad night and that he should continue to pursue a second term.

If you don’t think that played a role in the party’s putrid public standing, I really don’t know what to tell you.

I have seen a bunch of concern that Jill Biden releasing her book and bringing all of this back before the midterms will hurt Democrats in the election. I do not buy that. While it’s no fun for anyone to relive that time period, I can’t imagine that book causing a single person not to vote for a Democrat, let alone choose to vote for a Republican.

However, Democrats are still underperforming in part because people don’t really trust the party. In the most recent NYT poll, 79% of people who disapprove of Trump say they plan to vote for a Democrat in 2026. In 2018 and other elections, upwards of 90% of Trump disapprovers voted for a Democrat.

Is that specifically because of Biden? I can’t prove it, but common sense dictates that if people don’t trust the party to do what it says, then what happened in 2024 must be part of it.

And even if you fully dismiss the idea that the Biden fiasco in 2024 plays any role in the party’s current problems, you must admit that the party has a trust problem with the broader electorate and our own voters.

I believe that rebuilding that trust must be a priority for the party leadership. We need to lean into transparency, humility, and honest expectations setting about what is possible. This is why I have been so exercised about the DNC autopsy decision. I never thought that, even under the best-case scenario, a DNC autopsy would be some sort of Rosetta Stone for politics, uncovering something not covered in other post-election reports.

However, if you want to rebuild trust, you can’t promise an autopsy, refuse to release it, and then lie about the reasons why.

If you want to rebuild trust, the party leadership can’t pick a preferred candidate, lie about it, and then funnel millions of dollars through third-party vehicles to secretly help those candidates.

Democrats will probably do pretty well in these midterms despite our issues. Midterms are a referendum on the party in power. They aren’t really about the opposition, although we may not win as many seats as we otherwise would.

But in 2028, Democrats are about to embark on what will almost certainly be a very raucous, divisive presidential primary. We need voters to trust the process. And then in the general election for all of the marbles, we need voters to trust that our candidate will do what he or she says.

Rebuilding trust begins with admitting when we got something wrong. I wish Jill Biden had done that.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Can Graham Platner Still Win?
item.description: Graham Platner’s Senate campaign has been tumultuous, to say the least. Many months ago, it was rocked by revelations about very offensive things Platner had posted online earlier in his life. Just as that mini-scandal was on the cusp of being contained, new revelations came out about a…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Graham Platner’s Senate campaign has been tumultuous, to say the least. Many months ago, it was rocked by revelations about very offensive things Platner had posted online earlier in his life. Just as that mini-scandal was on the cusp of being contained, new revelations came out about a Nazi-adjacent tattoo that Platner claims he unknowingly got with his Marine buddies while on leave during the Iraq War.

Despite all of that baggage, Platner continued to surge in the polls — so much so that Janet Mills, the two-term incumbent governor with the full backing of the Democratic establishment, was forced to drop out of the race.

And now, on the cusp of officially securing the nomination, Platner and his campaign have once again been rocked by another set of disturbing revelations.

Over the weekend, the Wall Street Journal reported that:

Days after Graham Platner announced his Maine Senate bid, his wife informed the campaign about a potential political problem she had previously discovered on the oyster farmer’s phone: sexually explicit texts with several women, according to people familiar with the matter.

The new revelations sparked fears that Democrats may be about to blow a must-win Senate seat, and reignited the seemingly never-ending intra-Democratic Party fight around Graham Platner.

While this story is still unfolding, many are asking: can Graham Platner still win? Did Democrats make a mistake in nominating an untested outsider with scant political experience?

From the moment he got into the race, Graham Platner has been an incredibly polarizing figure within the Democratic coalition. To some, he is a model for the sort of candidate Democrats need to embrace, a populist outsider with working-class credentials. To others, he represents everything wrong with politics, and there is something immoral in embracing him. Online, at least, there is no middle ground. You are either passionately pro-Platner or vehemently anti-Platner. There is no room for nuance.

As is often true when there is a disproportionate online reaction, the Platner discourse is generally about much more than Platner himself. He has become a proxy for several other debates within the party, including its ideological direction, the future of the current Democratic establishment, and the party’s response to antisemitism on the Left.

None of these is particularly easy to unpack, but I’ll give it a shot.

First, Platner has sparked considerable antipathy among members of the Democratic establishment. They are the ones who were most critical after the latest revelations. There are several reasons for this. Platner is running explicitly and aggressively against the Democratic establishment, and Chuck Schumer in particular. The fact that he was able to dispatch a two-term incumbent governor who had been endorsed by the DSCC with such ease speaks to a dramatic shift in the party’s power dynamic. There are many echoes of the Bernie v. Hillary and Obama v. Hillary battles of the past, and some of Platner’s biggest critics are veterans of those fights. That’s not the only reason, but many of them were strongly opposed to Platner before any negative information surfaced.

Second, there is a question about whether Democrats are embracing a bad and toxic candidate just to win elections. Sarah Longwell made this argument on Twitter:

Dems watched Republicans excuse and justify Trump (and hundreds of candidates like him) for everything: sexual assault, racism, brazen corruption, lying about and trying to overturn an election. Of course the result is Dem voters offering the same expression of raw political power they’ve seen from Republicans.

Sarah is very smart, but I’m not sure I agree that’s what is happening. Maine Democrats really like Graham Platner, and I think they like him despite his transgressions, not because of them. Platner has been all over the state, holding town halls and meeting voters. His numbers went up, not down, after his toxic posts came out, not because voters liked what he posted, but because they bought his explanations and accepted his apologies. Could that change with the latest revelations? Of, course.

Third, of all of Platner’s issues, the “Nazi tattoo” looms the largest. That concern is understandable, especially given the very real rise of antisemitism, including on the Left. This is a very real issue and should not be dismissed. Platner has said he did not know about the Nazi connections to the totenkopf symbol when he got the tattoo with a bunch of his Marine buddies while on leave from the Iraq War. You can either buy that explanation or not. I do buy it, largely because there is no other evidence of Platner holding any Nazi-like views. And it’s not like Platner was shy about sharing his opinions online. Maine Democrats, at least, have heard about the tattoo, they have heard Platner’s explanation, and chose to support him anyway.

Finally, others have blamed Democrats’ fetishizing of outsiders for ending up with a candidate with Platner’s vulnerabilities. I have to say I find this argument woefully obtuse. It’s much more of a death rattle of the establishment than a coherent argument. It’s not that a bunch of pundits and Democratic consultants want outsider candidates, although many do. It’s that voters want outsiders. They think the political system is corrupt and that politicians are in it for themselves and their donors. And Democratic voters think their leadership is feckless and should be replaced. The dividing line in politics these days is insider v. outsider, which is why the winner of four of the last five presidential elections has been an outsider.

There is risk with outsiders. They are untested and may have vulnerabilities, but it’s not ipso facto true that every outsider has explicit texts and controversial tattoos. Rejecting all outsiders for insiders out of risk aversion would be a fatal error, and honestly sounds more like a last-ditch attempt by the establishment to hold onto power.

There is no credible path to a Senate majority without Maine. It’s the only solidly blue state with a Republican incumbent. So the stakes are incredibly high for Platner and everyone else who desperately wants a bulwark against Trump’s corrupt authoritarianism.

Beating Susan Collins is harder than it looks. She won reelection in 2020 by 8.5 points while Biden was winning the state by 9. And by dint of the size of the Republican majority, Collins has been able to avoid some of the most politically toxic votes, like the so-called Big Beautiful Bill and some of Trump’s most polarizing nominees.

Having said that, Maine is not only a very Democratic state — it’s more Democratic than it was the last time Collins was on the ballot. From 2020 to 2024, Maine’s population grew by 3%, and almost all of that growth came from people moving into the state. Most of the new population settled in the liberal stronghold of Portland and its suburbs. A good chunk of these new Mainers are the college-educated voters who have been a major part of the Democratic coalition since 2016.

Collins is also much less popular than she was in 2020. Back then, her approval was minus 6 points. Two polls in 2026 show Collins’ approval at nearly 20 points underwater.

It’s too early to know how the latest revelations will affect the race. More could come out. Historically, voters tend to shrug their shoulders at personal conduct like infidelity, presuming it is consensual and not sexual misconduct of the kind that ended Eric Swalwell’s campaign. But I don’t know, and neither does anyone else.

Some folks have analogized this situation to the 2020 North Carolina Senate race, when Democrat Cal Cunningham faced reports of inappropriate texts with a woman who was not his wife. It’s not clear to me that Cunningham would have won that race even without the scandal. Biden lost North Carolina, and almost universally, Democratic Senate candidates ran even with or slightly behind the top of the ticket. Those reports also came at the eleventh hour, while the ones involving Platner have surfaced five months before the election.

The risk to Platner here is that it raises questions about his redemption narrative. He and the campaign are correct that this is a private matter between a husband and a wife, but that private matter has now spilled into public view, and Platner will have to take it head-on to survive.

As we sit here today, Platner can clearly still win. He is probably a slight favorite to do so, given the demographics of the state, the political environment, and Collins’ unpopularity. But every new revelation ups the degree of difficulty, because Platner doesn’t just have to convince Democrats — he needs to convince Democrats who have voted for Collins in the past, often more than once.

And a divided Democratic Party certainly won’t make things easier.

I don’t know Graham Platner. I’ve never interviewed or met him. I do, however, know several people who have gotten to know him, and they paint a picture very different from what you see online. Graham himself has said he went through some very hard times after coming home from war and has grown and matured over the years. His version of his story is one of redemption. You can hate what Platner has done and said and support him today, if you believe his explanation and accept his apology.

For a lot of Democrats and the people of Maine, that is the big question: do you believe he has changed for the better? That his past will remain in the past?

And if not — are you willing to let Donald Trump and the Republicans control the Senate for the next two years?

There are no easy answers, and the good news is that I, and 99% of the people yelling about it on Twitter, have no actual say in the matter. It’s up to the people of Maine. They chose Platner as their nominee, and they will decide if he goes to the Senate.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Should Democrats Try to Expand the House?
item.description: Welcome to this week’s mailbag. So many questions this week, and so many of them are about the fallout from the Supreme Court ruling enabling racial gerrymandering. All of them dripping with existential dread — a sentiment I very much understand. Let’s get right into it, but first, some…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to this week’s mailbag. So many questions this week, and so many of them are about the fallout from the Supreme Court ruling enabling racial gerrymandering. All of them dripping with existential dread — a sentiment I very much understand. Let’s get right into it, but first, some housekeeping.

These mailbags run every Saturday as a special feature for paid subscribers. Subscribe to get full access and drop your questions for future mailbags.

And don’t forget to leave your questions for next week’s mailbag in the comments.

Dan, what impact do you think expanding the House to (population-of-US)/(population-of-Wyoming) representatives? This would have a big impact on the Electoral College, would probably make partisan gerrymandering a greater challenge (you’d end up splitting safe seats too finely to keep them safe?), and would just generally be more equitable (next stop: DC statehood).

In the wake of the Supreme Court ruling on gerrymandering, Democrats and others concerned about democracy have been looking for big structural solutions to a growing structural problem.

One idea getting a lot of discussion in certain corners is expanding the House. Unlike most other big democracy reforms — getting rid of the Electoral College, overturning Citizens United — changing the number of House members can be done legislatively. You do not need a constitutional amendment.

The 435 cap is set by the Permanent Apportionment Act of 1929. Congress could change it tomorrow with a simple majority in both chambers and a presidential signature. The only practical limitations set by the Constitution are that every state gets at least one representative and there can be no more than one representative per 30,000 people.

So it can be done. But is it a good idea, and should Democrats pursue it if and when we have a governing trifecta again?

The most common approach to House expansion is the “Wyoming Rule.” With its population of approximately 585,000, Wyoming is the smallest state, so it sets the floor under the constitutional requirement that every state get at least one member.

Under this rule, you would expand the House so that there is one member of Congress for every 585,000 people. The US population is about 335 million, so that would expand the House to about 575 members.

There are three reasons to consider House expansion: making the House more democratic, making gerrymandering harder, and reducing the Electoral College’s bias toward small states.

There is no question that House expansion would make representation more equitable. As it stands, Wyoming has one representative for 585,000 people, while Delaware and Rhode Island both have populations over a million and only one or two representatives respectively.

Expansion would also make gerrymandering more challenging. It’s harder to slice and dice districts when they’re smaller and more compact. It remains legal, however, and it will still happen.

The impact on the Electoral College is real but more limited than people often assume. According to the American Redistricting Project, this is what the Electoral College map would look like:

Expansion would narrow the popular-vote/Electoral-College gap by reducing the bias toward small states baked into the current system. But it would not have changed the result of any recent presidential election.

Theoretically, giving big states more influence would help Democrats because of Republican dominance in the Plains states. That advantage is largely cancelled out, however, by Republican strength in Texas and Florida — both of which will gain even more electoral votes in the next census.

In other words, House expansion is probably the right thing to do, but it doesn’t solve all of Democrats’ problems

Given the progressive win in Philadelphia’s race for the 3rd Congressional District (Chris Rabb beat the machine favorite and a popular newcomer) and the Working Families Party’s favorite candidate for Senate in Michigan (Abdul El Sayed) , do you think the Democratic Party will have to go to the far left? Will moderates be able to prevail with the surging popularity of the WFP and other left wing groups?

There’s no question that progressive candidates are winning some big primaries. We saw it with Mamdani in New York City and with Graham Platner pulling even with Janet Mills in the Maine Senate primary. Progressives have also notched wins in Arizona, New Jersey, and elsewhere.

There have been losses too. Nida Allam, a progressive Sanders-backed candidate, narrowly lost her North Carolina primary to a moderate incumbent.

It’s incredibly impressive that Rabb beat the machine in Philly — one of the last places in America where a political machine still really exists. But it’s also one of the most Democratic districts in the country, so I’m not sure we should overread it as a dramatic shift one way or the other.

The upcoming primaries in Iowa and Michigan will be better tests. The one thing I will say is this: voters — and Democratic voters especially — want big change. Progressives have been doing a better job of speaking to that anger and offering solutions commensurate with the problems.

If moderates want to win more elections, they need to adjust their agenda to meet the moment.

What are your thoughts on polling protest movement participation, I have seen a couple polls of such recently (and NBC/StarTrubune poll of Minnesota from February, and No Kings did one after their most recent protest), and was wondering if there is any history or context of these you could share?

Polling protest participation is more useful for historical comparison than for political analysis. Is participation as widespread as the anti-Vietnam or Iraq protests, the Civil Rights movement, or the protests after the murder of George Floyd in 2020?

The other use is measuring participation against the “3.5% rule.” This is a theory developed by political scientist Erica Chenoweth, who studied resistance movements from 1900 to 2006 and found that no government has withstood a sustained nonviolent movement that mobilized 3.5% of the population in active participation. When people wonder what it would take for a protest movement to bring down or stop the Trump Administration, that’s the number we’re looking for.

Dan, I know I’m looking backwards, but as the issue has returned to haunt us, can you elucidate the deeper story as to why in the world Ken Martin was chosen over Ben Wikler for DNC head? (And I don’t mean the perennial answer which is the dysfunction of the Democratic Party.)

I feel like I can’t stop talking about the DNC chair race. It’s a topic that is both incredibly important and pretty detached from the day-to-day of stopping Trump and winning elections. But it’s apparently a topic of endless fascination for Message Box subscribers, and my mantra is “the subscriber is always right.” (Basically, this newsletter is a digital Olive Garden.)

Before running for chair, Ken Martin was the Chair of the Minnesota Democratic Party — where he had real success — but he was also the chair of the Association of State Democratic Committees. That position let him build deep relationships with fellow chairs and vice chairs over many years. Here’s why that matters: to become DNC chair, you have to win a majority of the 448 voting members of the DNC. The 50 state party chairs and vice chairs, plus the chairs and vice chairs from 6 territories and Democrats Abroad, add up to more than 100 votes on their own.

That bloc was the foundation of Martin’s winning coalition. During the recent brouhaha over the autopsy, Martin’s defenders have tried to frame the criticism as coming from DC insiders who preferred Wikler. While it’s true that Ben had a lot of support from national Democratic types (I supported Ben), Ken won with a different set of insiders. He was the choice of the people who get checks from the DNC.

Why leave Ben Wikler out of your list of new DNC chair? Is he not interested? And would you have a new DNC chair do a new autopsy or has that ship sailed?

Okay, one more DNC question.

To be honest, I have no recollection of offering a list of potential successors. Not saying I didn’t — the last few months/years have been a bit of a blur. If I did, and I left Ben off, it would have been because Ben has told many people he has no interest in the role (though I haven’t asked him directly).

And yes, I would have the new chair complete the autopsy. It can still be useful.

Dan, what can be done to “nuke” (as Thom Tillis said) the $1.8B slush fund?

Lots of hard questions this week. This one is pretty easy. Congress could just pass a law banning it. Republican Brian Fitzpatrick and Democrat Tom Suozzi already have a bill that would do just that.

Will Congress pass it? Probably not. But I do think there’s a decent chance they will include some guardrails, transparency, and accountability measures in the reconciliation bill they’ll take up this month.

Can you Pod Save America guys and gals CREATE viral content versus advising others to do it?

You’re pros! What do you need from us all to grab the microphone and slow the prevailing right-wing media which brainwashes so many voters?

I promise you we are out there trying every single day. We're making content for every platform, in every format.

Okay, that’s it for this week. I am taking a little family time in the coming weeks, so not sure if there will be a mailbag the next two Saturdays.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: The Republicans Just Nominated the One Guy Who Could Lose Texas
item.description: The field in the Lone Star State is set. Last night, Texas Republicans nominated Trump-backed and scandal-plagued Attorney General Ken Paxton for Senate. Paxton defeated John Cornyn, a four-term incumbent and former Senate Majority Whip. This is an absolute gift to Democrats. The Paxton-Cornyn…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
The field in the Lone Star State is set. Last night, Texas Republicans nominated Trump-backed and scandal-plagued Attorney General Ken Paxton for Senate. Paxton defeated John Cornyn, a four-term incumbent and former Senate Majority Whip.

This is an absolute gift to Democrats.

The Paxton-Cornyn primary was one of the most brutal and expensive in modern political history, with accusations of adultery, bribery, support for Sharia law, and so much else. The end result is a bruised and battered, very vulnerable Ken Paxton running against an incredibly talented and well-funded Democrat in James Talarico.

Texas has been the great white whale for Democrats. We have been eyeing the state's shifting demographics, waiting for the day when we had a real shot at turning it blue. Every few cycles, we get our hopes up, fall in love with an exciting Texas Democrat, invest our money and hopes into Texas, only to have the rug pulled out in the end.

So, I understand why so many people are skeptical of Talarico’s chances. This is a very hard state. But make no mistake: this is Democrats’ best shot to win Texas in a generation.

The core strategic debate in the Democratic Party has been whether the best way to win is by motivating our base to maximize turnout or persuading swing voters. This is ultimately a false choice, but the answer could not be clearer in Texas.

Donald Trump won Texas by nearly 14 points in 2024. To win, a Democrat is going to need to persuade a significant number of people who pulled the lever for Trump to switch sides. That means a candidate who can persuade voters with whom they have significant disagreements.

This is James Talarico’s specialty. He has centered his campaign on going into hostile spaces and reaching out to the opposition. He even did one of those Jubilee debates where he sat down and debated 20 undecided voters.

Talarico is a proud Christian who attends seminary and talks about his faith on the campaign trail. He often uses his Christianity as a way to connect with voters who disagree with him on policy.

This is not to say he doesn’t have vulnerabilities. He has a number of statements from the 2020 era that are already being weaponized against him.

But I have watched him from afar and up close for a number of years, and he is a uniquely talented politician who might as well have been designed in a lab to give Democrats their best chance to win Texas in a very long time. At a time when voters are skeptical of Democrats, James Talarico seems like the opposite of a typical Democrat.

Look, John Cornyn isn’t exactly a political colossus. He’s a pretty bad campaigner. He looks ridiculous in a cowboy hat and might as well have “Republican Establishment” tattooed on his forehead.

But Paxton is a uniquely terrible candidate.

He was reported to the FBI for bribery by eight of his own staffers and was impeached for bribery by the Republicans in the Texas House — not exactly a bunch of woke MS-NOW-watching libs.

Paxton’s wife — a Republican state senator — filed for divorce last year on “biblical grounds,” citing adultery. He was indicted on felony securities fraud in 2015 and only resolved it in 2024 through a pretrial diversion deal. The real estate developer at the center of the bribery allegations against him later pleaded guilty to federal bank fraud charges. The Republican-controlled Texas House impeached him 121-33. He spoke at the Ellipse on January 6 and led the lawsuit trying to throw out votes in four states Biden won.

Every single one of these things is real, and the GOP nominated him anyway.

This six-minute video from the Cornyn campaign walks through Paxton’s vulnerabilities in lurid detail:

And the GOP base isn’t sold on him either. In the most recent UT/Texas Politics Project poll, only 63% of Texas Republicans said they’d vote for Paxton over Talarico. One in six Republicans said they’d vote for Talarico or someone else over their own party’s nominee.

To be fair, the polling is a little all over the map, but Talarico tends to lead Paxton by more than Cornyn in hypothetical matchups. There is clearly a swath of Texans who would vote for John Cornyn over James Talarico but are open to voting for Talarico over Paxton.

Something bigger than the strength of the individual candidates is going on here.

Back in the mid-2010s, Democrats had real hope for Texas based on the state’s growing Latino population. Texas was the rare state where Hillary Clinton improved on Barack Obama’s 2012 performance. Latinos were voting overwhelmingly for Democrats and were a rapidly growing share of the Texas electorate. The math seemed inexorable. Before too long, the state would be blue.

That started to change in 2020 and took a dramatic turn for the worse in 2024. According to the 2024 exit polls, Trump won Texas Latinos by 10 points.

But Latinos have shifted dramatically away from Republicans in the 18 months since. Nationally, Trump’s numbers with Latino voters are in the toilet, deeply underwater, with disapproval running 30+ points ahead of approval in recent national polling.

The Latino retrenchment is showing up at the ballot box. In New Jersey and Virginia, Latinos swung massively toward Democrats in 2025.

In Texas, Democrat Taylor Rehmet flipped a state Senate seat Trump carried by 17 points. Precincts with a majority of Hispanic residents swung on average 34 percentage points toward Rehmet. And according to DCCC analysis, turnout in the March 2026 primary in 46 majority-Hispanic counties was 33 percent higher than in the 2024 primaries — and Democratic ballots made up 66 percent of votes cast in those counties.

If these shifts persist on Election Day in November, it will be a massive boon to Talarico.

It’s not just Latinos, either. Trump himself is now underwater in Texas. The Silver Bulletin reported over the weekend that Trump’s approval has gone negative in the state he won by 14 points 18 months ago. He has nothing left in the tank to lend Paxton.

If this level of optimism feels eerily familiar, it’s because it was only back in 2018 when America caught a case of Beto-fever. Beto O’Rourke became a national sensation as he tried to take down Ted Cruz. He raised tens of millions and got support from celebrities like Beyoncé. His campaign was a genuine cultural phenomenon.

In the end, Beto fell short, losing to Cruz by 2.6 points.

Now look at the differences between then and now:

In September of 2018, Quinnipiac found Ted Cruz with a 52/43 favorability. The most recent net favorability number we have for Paxton with Texas voters is -5. Cruz was a popular(ish) incumbent. Paxton is an unpopular nominee with a divorce on biblical grounds, a federal bribery investigation, an impeachment, and a securities fraud indictment in his rearview mirror.

In 2018, Trump’s approval in Texas was hovering around even. Today, he’s underwater in Texas and at the lowest point of his entire presidency nationally.

In 2018, Latinos were drifting toward Republicans. Today, they’re swinging back hard.

In 2018, Democrats had a fantastic candidate facing a competent Republican incumbent who was personally popular. In 2026, Democrats have a fantastic candidate facing a scandal-plagued challenger his own party tried to destroy.

Texas is a tough state. This will not be easy. Democrats need things to break our way. But we have a uniquely talented candidate running against a uniquely damaged opponent in an environment that is, on every single measurable dimension, more favorable than the year we came within 200,000 votes.

That’s as good a shot as we’re going to get.

I, for one, choose to be optimistic.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Mailbag Questions, Take Two
item.description: Due to a technical glitch exacerbated by user error, comments were disabled on Saturday’s mailbag post, preventing people from submitting their questions for next week. They were also disabled on several other recent posts. To be clear, this was not intentional — comments somehow got set to…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Due to a technical glitch exacerbated by user error, comments were disabled on Saturday’s mailbag post, preventing people from submitting their questions for next week. They were also disabled on several other recent posts. To be clear, this was not intentional — comments somehow got set to “disabled” by default, and I didn’t catch it.

Comments are back on.

I’ll try to be more careful going forward. In the meantime, feel free to drop your questions in the comments below (and to chastise me for botching yesterday’s post, or anything else).

item.title: If Trump Is So Unpopular, Why Do His Candidates Keep Winning?
item.description: Welcome to this week’s mailbag. So many questions this week, and a lot of them came in on Tuesday night and Wednesday morning after a Trump-backed candidate easily beat Representative Thomas Massie in the Kentucky primary. Folks want to know why Trump-backed candidates keep winning primaries…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to this week’s mailbag. So many questions this week, and a lot of them came in on Tuesday night and Wednesday morning after a Trump-backed candidate easily beat Representative Thomas Massie in the Kentucky primary. Folks want to know why Trump-backed candidates keep winning primaries despite Trump being so unpopular. It’s a great question, and I want to get right into it, but first, some housekeeping.

These mailbags run every Saturday as a special feature for paid subscribers. Subscribe to get full access and drop your questions for future mailbags.

And don’t forget to leave your questions for next week’s mailbag in the comments.

So you and other pundits keep telling us how unpopular Trump is, how it’s going to be a backlash, but then in primaries the people he backs keep winning! It feels like his unpopularity isn’t actually showing up in what’s happening on the ground. What do you make of this?

Look, I get it. I, too, tuned in on the night of the Indiana and Kentucky primaries, hoping for Trump to get his comeuppance, and left disappointed to see him win yet again. Like all of you, I was hoping for a sign that Republicans were ready to truly turn on Trump.

Alas, that was not the case. But Trump-backed candidates winning primaries is not a positive sign for Republicans heading into the fall. In fact, it may portend the opposite.

Trump is very unpopular. He is less popular than almost any president at this point in their term. This chart from the New York Times shows just how unpopular he is.

The remarkable drop in Trump’s approval is because the voters who flocked to him in 2024 have abandoned him. In the recent NYT/Siena poll, he is 51 points underwater with Latino voters, 57 points underwater with voters under 30, and 43 points underwater with Independents.

So why do Trump-backed candidates keep winning? Because they are winning Republican primaries, and Trump is still very popular among Republicans. In the NYT poll, he has an 82% approval rating with Republicans. His numbers are probably much higher among the more hard-core Republicans who vote in primaries, and higher still among those in deeply red states like Indiana, Kentucky, and Louisiana. Trump won Indiana by 19 points, Louisiana by 22 points, and Thomas Massie’s district by more than 30 points.

And not for nothing, but an 82% approval rating among Republicans is nothing to write home about. It’s down about 10 points since the start of his term.

These wins tell us almost nothing about what will happen in November. A Republican president spending millions of dollars and tons of political capital to beat incumbent Republicans in very Republican areas is just not that impressive.

As I wrote last week, I think Trump’s vengeance tour is going to hurt Republicans in the midterms because it makes it harder for them to break from a deeply unpopular president.

Dan, in a nightmare scenario for those trying to change the old views of the senior Democrats, is there a world where Talarico wins in Texas but Platner loses in Maine? Is such a thing even remotely possible? Would that set those of us trying to have more outsiders enter into politics have a strong setback (not that Talarico is some major Washington insider, but he appears to have more backing by traditional Democrats)?

It would be pretty unusual for Democrats to lose a state Kamala Harris won by 7 points and win one that Trump won by 14 points. Anything is possible, but that would require a historic meltdown from Platner.

That said, I very much agree with your concern. I addressed this exact point in a recent interview with the New York Times. Here’s what I said:

Whether or not [Platner] beats Collins is going to have huge implications for the party. Obviously, Democrats aren’t getting the majority without winning a blue state like Maine. But if Platner wins, it will widen the aperture for the kinds of candidates Democrats are open to and may change how risk-averse we should be when looking at potential 2028 nominees. Will we play it safe with a traditional politician or be willing to embrace an outsider who can upend the traditional political calculus?

The DCCC and the DSCC should make it a rule to stay out of primaries, perhaps with some nuance, and let the voters choose their candidates. It would show leadership, trust in voters, and be the right little-d democratic thing to do. Heck, the party should push for all primaries to be rank choice and really shift to doing all the things we think make for better, fairer elections where those are reasonable within their control.

I generally agree with the sentiment, because I don’t really trust many of the folks in Washington to make the right choices. There are, however, times when the party needs to step in to make sure we don’t end up with really bad, clearly less-electable candidates. We are seeing this play out in Texas, where a Democratic House candidate named Maureen Gallindo is in a runoff against another Democrat. She has called for turning ICE facilities into camps to imprison Zionists and castrate pedophiles (whom she assumes are mostly Zionists). Gallindo’s antisemitic and generally unhinged comments are a perfect example of when the party has to intervene.

How has the redistricting upheaval, SCOTUS rulings and state level election threat to house democratic congressional seats indirectly impacted the democratic path to senate majority that was just behinning to materialize before all of this?

The various terrible court rulings only impact the House map, because they specifically refer to the drawing of House districts. The Democratic path to the Senate remains the same, with a majority definitely within reach. If Ken Paxton wins the Texas primary next week, that will help our chances of winning Texas. I also think there could be a backlash to the clearly racist gerrymandering that leads to increased Democratic turnout across the country.

The democrats need 214 seats in the House of Representatives to gain a majority. Is that still possible with all the gerrymandering?

Very much so. Given the narrow GOP majority, Trump’s unpopularity, and the overall political environment, Democrats are still the favorites to take the House. Gerrymandering limits the size of that majority. Democrats won 41 seats in 2018, and the political environment right now appears better than it was then. But we will win fewer seats this time because there are fewer seats in play and Republicans have eliminated a number of safe Democratic seats.

In your PSA interview with Axelrod, you said involvement with the Biden Administration would be net neutral at best. Did you mean in a general election? A candidate who runs against Biden and his administration won’t be nominated in ‘28.

I meant in both the general and the primary. I don’t think anyone should run against Biden, although there are parts of his administration — most notably his Gaza policy — that I imagine every candidate will run against. The discussion focused on Pete Buttigieg, his service in the Biden Administration, and his role as Transportation Secretary. I am pretty skeptical that it will be particularly helpful to him. I don’t think voters see that job as preparatory for the presidency. I don’t really think it is going to hurt him either. He will face some questions about Biden’s decision to run again, but I’m not sure how relevant those questions will be to voters by 2027. And I don’t think anyone believes the Transportation Secretary was in the inner circle making decisions about the reelection.

As we sit here today, Joe Biden remains deeply unpopular with the larger electorate. That could change, but if it doesn’t, being associated with that administration just isn’t that helpful. Hence, my assessment that it was net neutral at best.

Will Schumer and Jeffries face serious competition in the party leadership voting in the next Congress?

Based on all of the reporting, Jeffries seems to be safe. All bets are off if Democrats fail to take the House, but if they do, Jeffries will almost certainly be the next Speaker. I think that’s a good thing. He’s not perfect, but Jeffries has handled himself pretty well. He navigated both shutdowns with skill and strength, held his party together, and has been aggressive in fighting back against the GOP gerrymandering. It’s not his fault that the Virginia map got tossed or that the Supreme Court greenlit racial gerrymandering.

Schumer is more of an open question. There is clearly a group of Democratic senators displeased with aspects of Schumer’s leadership and skeptical of his understanding of modern politics. The Janet Mills fiasco is an example of what they are concerned with. It’s also fair to say that he has recruited some great candidates in Ohio, North Carolina, and Alaska. If the Democrats fail to take the Senate, he could be in jeopardy. If they do, I would be surprised to see someone defeat him — or even run against him.

Schumer is up in 2028. Many people assume he won’t run for reelection, and there is some sense that his colleagues may let him finish out his term as leader.

Time will tell.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: Ken Martin Has to Go
item.description: If you’ve been following politics over the last month or so, you are more than familiar with the saga over the autopsy that the DNC was refusing to release. After stonewalling for months and an incredibly viral interview on Pod Save America, where DNC Chair Ken Martin vigorously and incredulously…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
If you’ve been following politics over the last month or so, you are more than familiar with the saga over the autopsy that the DNC was refusing to release. After stonewalling for months and an incredibly viral interview on Pod Save America, where DNC Chair Ken Martin vigorously and incredulously defended the decision not to release the autopsy, the whole imbroglio has roiled the party for weeks and led to a debate about the debate.

All along, Martin’s argument was that the autopsy had happened, it had been helpful in informing his strategy, but that releasing it would be a distraction and divide the party before the midterms.

Well, that was all bullshit.

CNN got their hands on several parts of the autopsy, which forced the DNC to release the rest of it to CNN and the world. Here’s what was

In other words, the autopsy wasn’t released because the political crony that Martin hired to do the job didn’t really do the job. He failed to speak to many of the top decision makers and never really wrestled with the tough questions like Biden’s decision to run for reelection, his age, or the impact of his Gaza policy.

The final product was a joke. Not good enough to be released, so Ken Martin just lied about it until he got caught.

This is the final straw.

Ken Martin is not up to the job, and his continued presence is going to make the DNC woefully ineffective heading into the midterms and then the critical 2028 primaries, where the DNC plays a major role.

He should step down for the good of the party, and if he won’t, the DNC should fire him.

Too Many Bricks on the Load

I will lay my cards on the table. I supported Ben Wikler over Ken Martin for DNC chair. I am friends with Ben, but also think he is the best party chair in the country and would have done a stellar job running the DNC. But I am not anti-Ken Martin. I know lots of people praise the work he did running the party in Minnesota. I mostly had sympathy for him. Under the best circumstances, running the DNC is a miserable task. These are far from the best circumstances.

However, the autopsy fiasco shows why Ken Martin should not be the DNC Chair.

First, Martin could not have managed this more poorly. He didn’t pick a qualified person to run the autopsy. The fact that he was apparently shocked by the work product shows there was no oversight of the process. Once it became clear that the autopsy was not ready for prime time, he turned what should have been a bump in the road into a major distraction. It’s hard to see how Martin could have screwed this up worse. According to the reporting, Martin is insular, overly defensive, and paranoid.

If you can’t do the small things, you can’t do the big things.

As he implicitly admits in a blog post, Martin lied to everyone for months about why he wasn’t releasing it. Here’s what Ken

The thing that is so bizarre and disturbing about his approach is that Martin leaned into the lies. His Pod Save America interview didn’t come out of the blue. We didn’t invite him on and ambush him. He started complaining to my cohosts about how we have discussed the autopsy and wanted to come on to make his case. He wanted to come and lie to everyone about the autopsy.

Why would anyone trust Ken Martin after this?

We need people to trust the DNC. We are about to embark on a highly consequential, and likely contentious, presidential primary, in which the DNC will play a major role. They set the primary calendar, decide how delegates are awarded, sponsor the debates, and put on the convention.

If no one trusts the DNC, no one will trust the process set up by the DNC. And that will make it harder to unite the party around the eventual nominee.

Ken Martin said his job is winning elections. Well, the best way for Martin to help Democrats win elections will be to step aside and give the DNC a fresh start.

This gives me no pleasure. I feel bad for the people at the DNC, and I even feel a little bad for Ken. But the stakes are too high. We need to put our best foot forward. Too much damage has been done.

Ken Martin has to go.

item.title: How to Beat the GOP's Midterm Playbook
item.description: Hi everyone As many of you may have heard, a few weeks ago I launched Message Box Pro, a subscription product for people who work in communications or politics at any level — from candidates to staffers to local organizers and activists. This is a project I’ve been working on for years, and I’m…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Hi everyone

As many of you may have heard, a few weeks ago I launched Message Box Pro, a subscription product for people who work in communications or politics at any level — from candidates to staffers to local organizers and activists. This is a project I’ve been working on for years, and I’m genuinely excited about how it’s coming together.

Today, I wanted to give you a real sense of what Message Box Pro members get in their inbox every week.

I’m offering Message Box subscribers a special deal: sign up and your first month is free. After that, you’ll be charged $65/month for full access, but only if you stay. Cancel anytime, no questions asked.

Thanks,

Dan

After Democrats romped in the 2025 elections, Republicans declared that their strategy for the midterms would be focused on affordability. White House Deputy Chief of Staff James Blair, Trump’s de facto political hand, told Politico:

The president is very keyed into what’s going on, and he recognizes, like anybody, that it takes time to do an economic turnaround, but all the fundamentals are there, and I think you’ll see him be very, very focused on prices and cost of living.

Trump was supposed to go on an “Affordability Tour” where he barnstormed the nation talking about the tax cuts in the Big Beautiful Bill and other (pretty fake) administration measures to lower costs. Of course, his pivot to an affordability message was an abject failure even before he launched his disastrous war in the Middle East. He can’t bring himself to admit prices are up, because doing so would mean admitting he failed to keep his core campaign promise. He gets visibly angry at the mere idea of affordability. He’s called it bullshit and immediately tells everyone how high the stock market is and how things have never been better.

This graph from Nate Silver shows just how poorly that strategy has worked.

The Republicans have lost on affordability. With gas prices rising and unlikely to fall significantly before the midterms, it’s hard to see what they could say or do that would change the dynamic.

So they are pivoting once again to a new midterm strategy. Here’s how Politico Playbook described it last week:

In other words, the GOP plans to use its massive financial advantage in this election to nuke the Democrats and make them an unacceptable alternative.

I am in no way surprised that the GOP landed here. It’s really the only option available to them. Given that Trump is now underwater on immigration, they can’t even pivot to his favorite issue. This is their best card in a bad hand.

They kind of have no viable options.

I should note that in this context — for most voters, at least — “woke” doesn’t mean using your pronouns, DEI, CRT, or any of the other stuff that drives Fox News programming. It’s just another way of saying “liberal.” The real message is that Democrats are dangerously out of the cultural mainstream and too weak to protect you.

The GOP has some things going for it. First, people do not love the Democratic Party. It remains quite unpopular — and in most polling, more unpopular than the Republican Party despite Trump’s abysmal approval rating.

The “Woke, Weak, and Way Too Liberal” message also fits with voters’ preconceived notions about the Democratic Party. A post-2024 election survey conducted by the centrist think tank Third Way asked voters to describe Kamala Harris and Donald Trump in one or two words.

You can see the results.

The fact that voters thought Trump was stronger and viewed Kamala Harris as too liberal is one reason he won and she lost. But Dan, don’t you always say Trump won because of inflation?

Good catch. The perception of Democrats as too liberal and too weak is a key reason voters didn’t trust Harris to tackle the problem of inflation.

Without any better options, the GOP is re-running its 2024 playbook.

I know this causes a lot of us to want to repeatedly bang our heads against the nearest hard surface. To a lot of swing voters in 2024, Trump didn’t seem “extreme.” He said he would protect Social Security and Medicare. He demurred on abortion. He didn’t code as a right-wing cultural warrior.

Meanwhile, because of a post-2020 backlash to progressivism, decisions made by the Biden administration on immigration, and a gazillion dollars in ads highlighting some of Kamala Harris’s most extreme positions, Democrats were the ones seen as extreme and culturally out of step.

A lot has changed since 2024. Trump is not the president most voters thought he would be. He and the Republicans are now seen as more extreme. In that CBS poll, 58% of voters describe Republicans as extreme. A Cook Political Report poll of the 32 battleground districts found that voters — and especially independents — are more concerned about Republican extremism than Democratic extremism.

On the other hand, the perception of Democrats as weak has not improved. In the same February CBS poll, nearly two-thirds of voters describe the party as weak. These numbers are driven, in part, by Democrats who see their own party leadership as too weak to stand up to Trump — but who are still planning to vote for Democrats this fall.

Two pieces of good news. First, strength is a much bigger issue in presidential or gubernatorial races than in legislative ones. Voters want their chief executive to be strong enough to protect them from threats at home and abroad. Second, Trump is no longer seen as a particularly strong figure. A recent TIPP poll found that a plurality of voters see Trump’s leadership as weak.

In other words, the GOP is trying to re-run its 2024 strategy. But Trump and the Republicans are in a very different place than they were in 2024.

The operatives running the GOP and the AI/Crypto-funded Super PACs know their only option is to nuke the Democratic brand. And even if that doesn’t fully work, it could help rally a deflated GOP base by raising the stakes of the election. The strategy may be the only one available to them, but the money behind it makes it dangerous regardless.

This assault affects everyone in a tough race with a D next to their name — whether or not your face is in the ad.

So the question is: what do you do about it?

First, this goes back to the Message Box exercise from the first memo. A good political or communications campaign must know what the other side plans to say about them. It’s particularly helpful when the other side announces their playbook in Politico, so we can prepare.

Second, if the other side is looking for ways to damage the Democratic brand, be more than a generic Democrat. (I plan to write more about this in the coming weeks.) Find ways to break from your party — by tacking to the middle, to the left, or by criticizing the party establishment, nationally or locally, where appropriate.

Third, win the battle on extremism. Politics is often a fight to claim the mantle of mainstream American values. The Republican Party has given us ample opportunity to paint them as the extreme ones. Don’t hesitate to do so.

Finally, the conventional wisdom in Democratic circles after 2024 was that the answer to “Democrats are too liberal” is to moderate ideologically. There’s something to that. Running as a generic progressive in a Trump +5 district is not a winning strategy.

But there’s an interesting wrinkle in some recent polling worth flagging. A February analysis by Strength In Numbers found that voter perceptions of Democratic strength were a stronger predictor of vote choice than perceptions of Democratic extremism.

I’d be careful about leaning too hard on a single data point. I don’t think this lets Democrats off the hook on the substantive policy questions about where the party should be positioned. But it does suggest something I’ve been arguing for years: how you fight matters as much as what you fight for. Voters reward candidates who project strength, fight for their constituents, and refuse to be pushed around — regardless of where they sit on the ideological spectrum.

For a downballot candidate facing the “woke, weak, and way too liberal” assault, the takeaway isn’t necessarily to soften your positions. It’s to project strength about whatever your positions actually are. The voters who will decide your race want to know you’ll fight for them.

Run your campaigns. Don’t swerve out of your lane, but look for opportunities to blunt the attack to come.

If you liked what you read and you want more strategy memos, messaging guidance, polling insights, and access to an incredible community of candidates, staffers, and organizers, sign up for Message Box Pro today and get your first month free.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post.

item.title: Everyone Thinks Trump Won Last Night. They're Wrong
item.description: Trump ran on a platform of lowering costs, securing the border, and cracking down on immigration. But after a year and a half, it’s clear that’s not really why Trump ran for a return to the White House. We know he doesn’t give two shits about lowering costs. He says “affordability” is bullshit and…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Trump ran on a platform of lowering costs, securing the border, and cracking down on immigration. But after a year and a half, it’s clear that’s not really why Trump ran for a return to the White House. We know he doesn’t give two shits about lowering costs. He says “affordability” is bullshit and calls the massive gas price spike “peanuts.” He obviously hates immigrants and enjoys the cruel and unconstitutional mass deportation. But Trump’s true passion — his top priority, the reason he gets out of bed in the morning — is to exact revenge on his enemies. (His home renovation projects, like the ballroom, might be a close second.)

The Trump Administration has spent a ton of wasted effort over the last year trying to prosecute Trump’s enemies. They have tried and failed to indict Adam Schiff, Jerome Powell, Letitia James, and Fed Governor Lisa Cook. Trump did succeed in bullying his Justice Department into indicting former FBI Director Jim Comey for threatening him with a seashell photo. But the Comey case seems highly likely to be tossed by any sane judge.

Where Trump had more success was in exacting political revenge on those who dared cross him. A few weeks ago, Trump’s political operation invested millions of dollars in defeating many of the Indiana State Senators who resisted his push to redraw their Congressional maps. Then, on Saturday, he helped defeat Louisiana Senator Bill Cassidy for voting to impeach Trump in 2021. Trump endorsed Congresswoman Julia Letlow in the race, and she came in first in the primary. Cassidy dropped all the way to third, meaning he doesn’t make the runoff — a brutal and embarrassing result for an incumbent Senator who had spent the last few years debasing himself to get back into Trump’s good graces.

Last night, Kentucky Congressman Thomas Massie lost his primary to a Trump-backed opponent. Trump has had Massie in his sights for years, but the thing that truly infuriated him was Massie’s work to force the release of the Epstein Files.

All of these wins have been a display of brute political force, and it has impressed some in the media. As an example, here is the reaction to the Massie defeat from Rachel Bade, a former Politico Playbook author turned Substacker.

Yes, the incumbent President can influence primaries in his own party. And yes, Trump is getting rid of the people he believes wronged him. But spending nearly $20 million to defeat a Republican in a district Trump won by 35 points is not the flex people think it is.

At best, this is a Pyrrhic victory — and Trump’s revenge tour is a big problem for the GOP moving forward.

The message to Republicans from Kentucky, Louisiana, and elsewhere is crystal clear — buck Trump and lose your job.

The problem for Republicans on the ballot this fall is that the best way to keep their job might be to buck Trump.

Trump’s approval rating is under 40%. He is embroiled in a deeply unpopular war. Voters hate the economy and blame Trump’s economic policies. The voters who made up his winning coalition have abandoned him.

Trump is a massive drag on his party, and to win, many Republicans will need to show some independence from the deeply unpopular President.

Trump won’t let them do that. Cross him, and he could turn on you. It’s too late for Trump to run a primary challenge against someone now, but he could attack them on social media, depressing turnout among the MAGA base, or cut off funding from his array of well-heeled Super PACs.

We are seeing this dynamic play out in the Senate, where Trump is trying to force Republicans to take the politically suicidal step of spending upwards of a billion taxpayer dollars on his asinine ballroom vanity project.

A smarter, savvier, less megalomaniacal leader would give his party the room to do what they need to win.

Trump is incapable of doing so, and the GOP will pay a price for it.

Everyone with half a brain in American politics believes Republicans would have a much better chance of winning the Texas Senate race with John Cornyn at the top of the ticket.

Cornyn is far from the best candidate, but he is better than scandal-plagued Ken Paxton. After Cornyn beat expectations in the primary, Trump said he would endorse a candidate, and everyone assumed that candidate would be Cornyn.

But then Trump didn’t do it for weeks. He let Cornyn twist in the wind and let Paxton regain momentum.

And then, yesterday morning, Trump said he would make an endorsement later in the day. Once again, everyone assumed it would be Cornyn. It was the logical move. A Trump endorsement would have likely put Cornyn over the top and positioned the GOP to hold onto a must-win Senate seat.

But no. Trump stunned everyone, including Republican Senators, by endorsing Paxton. In the endorsement post, Trump explained that he passed on Cornyn because

John was very late in backing me in what turned out to be a Historic Run for the Republican Nomination, and then, the Presidency, itself, both of which were Landslide Victories and, more importantly, gave us the Country that we have today.

Trump was more interested in seeking revenge than in helping Republicans hold onto the Senate.

The problem with defeating incumbents in primaries is that they don’t immediately leave Congress. They stay in their seats until January of next year. In addition to Cassidy, Trump also forced North Carolina Senator Thom Tillis into retirement. Now the two of them are hanging around the Senate, unburdened by any political need to appease Trump. Earlier this year, Tillis held up the nomination of Trump’s Fed Chair pick to force the DOJ to drop its bogus investigation into Jerome Powell.

And yesterday, Cassidy flipped his vote to allow the War Powers Resolution on the Iran War to pass the Senate — a stunning move that will cause some real headaches for Trump.

So sure, Trump has had some short-term wins in these primary contests. But these are wins that he, and the Republicans, will likely come to regret.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post.

item.title: The New NYT Poll Is Devastating News for Republicans
item.description: Ever since the Supreme Court decision allowing the GOP to eliminate Democratic districts across the South, Republicans have been feeling newly confident about the midterms. James Blair, Trump’s political honcho, even did an interview with Politico patting himself on the back for how Republicans had…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Ever since the Supreme Court decision allowing the GOP to eliminate Democratic districts across the South, Republicans have been feeling newly confident about the midterms. James Blair, Trump’s political honcho, even did an interview with Politico patting himself on the back for how Republicans had rigged the maps and laying out a “strategy” for keeping the House in the fall.

Many Democrats have reverted to our natural state — panic. You can feel the political narrative changing in real time.

Republicans are once again ascendant, with a real shot at surviving what should otherwise be a brutal midterm.

Well, the New York Times put out a devastating new poll this morning that shows the GOP’s newfound bravado is very much misplaced. The NYT poll is the highest-rated and most respected poll in the business. It’s not flawless, but it has a great track record, and it confirms what is clear when you ignore the noise and look at the fundamentals.

The Republican collapse has begun.

The New York Times poll makes it clear: Donald Trump’s political position is historically putrid, and he is an anvil around the neck of his party as we head into the midterms.

His overall approval rating is 38%, which confirms what we have seen in other recent polls. That’s terrible news for Trump and every Republican on the ballot — but when you dig into the crosstabs, the story actually gets worse.

Here is Trump’s approval among key groups:

  • Latino voters: 20/71 (-51)

  • Voters aged 18-29: 19/76 (-57)

  • Independents: 26/69 (-43)

It wasn’t that long ago that Republicans were talking about a semi-permanent majority built on Trump’s 2024 gains with Latinos, young voters, and less politically engaged independents.

Today’s poll shows that was a fleeting pipe dream at best. Those voters have turned on Trump with ferocity.

The most devastating number of all might be that his approval rating is only 79% among voters who backed Trump in 2024. Midterms are won or lost on a party’s ability to turn out a high percentage of its presidential-year coalition. Trump voters being unhappy with Trump is a very bad sign for the GOP.

Trump’s numbers were way down well before the Iran War, but there is no question that he and the Republicans are suffering from the backlash to that decision.

Iran is the accelerant. It’s the through-line of the whole poll, and almost certainly what moved the generic ballot:

  • Nearly two-thirds of voters say going to war was the wrong decision.

  • 64% disapprove of the war.

  • 55% say it won’t be worth the costs.

  • 52% don’t want operations to resume even if no deal is reached with the Iranians; only 37% do.

  • 50% think the administration will fail to eliminate Iran’s nuclear program.

Republicans are largely — but not entirely — sticking with Trump on the war: 23% of GOP voters disapprove of the war, and 22% think going to war with Iran was the wrong decision. The problem is worse among 2024 Trump voters, 28% of whom disapprove of the war.

Ultimately, the biggest problem for Trump is that people do not like the economy and believe his policies are making their economic problems worse. A near-majority of the public rates the economy as poor.

Again, the crosstabs are even worse for Trump. More than eight in ten Latinos, voters under 30, and independents rate the economy negatively, and 72% of white voters without a college degree, the core of Trump’s base, say the same.

Bad economies are bad for incumbent parties. The situation here is much worse. Voters blame Trump for their financial woes: 44% say his policies have hurt them personally. Even Trump voters are evenly split on whether his policies have helped them.

The most important finding — and the one causing panic among Republicans — is that Democrats have a 10-point advantage on the generic ballot. Among subgroups:

  • Voters under 30: Democrats +40

  • Latinos: Democrats +30

  • Independents: Democrats +18

  • People who didn’t vote in 2024: Democrats +31

Even 8% of Trump 2024 voters plan to vote for a Democrat in 2026. Given how aggressively Republicans have drawn new districts in Florida and Texas, the massive Democratic advantage with Latinos should cause real concern.

But that’s not even the worst news for Republicans.

According to the poll, the Democrats’ 10-point lead on the generic ballot is among registered voters. Among those most certain to vote, the Democratic lead jumps to 14 points.

That 14-point number is the one that matters. It's the electorate that actually shows up in a midterm. It tells you everything about why Republicans should be panicking instead of bragging.

Thanks for subscribing to  The Message Box . Please help spread the word by sharing this post with your network. Thanks!

item.title: How Dems Win the Gerrymandering War
item.description: Welcome to this week’s mailbag. So many questions this week from subscribers about the rapid and ongoing Republican effort to rig the midterms by redrawing congressional maps across the South — what it means for this year and what Democrats can do going forward. It’s hard to think of a more urgent…
item.author: Dan Pfeiffer from The Message Box
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to this week’s mailbag. So many questions this week from subscribers about the rapid and ongoing Republican effort to rig the midterms by redrawing congressional maps across the South — what it means for this year and what Democrats can do going forward.

It’s hard to think of a more urgent topic, so let’s get right into it.

But first, a little housekeeping:

These mailbags run every Saturday as a special feature for paid subscribers. Subscribe to get full access and drop your questions for future mailbags.

And don’t forget to leave your questions for next week’s mailbag in the comments.

I’m very concerned about the midterms after the Virginia ruling. Do you have any insight into how the Democratic Party plans to respond or shift strategy?

Do you think that Democrats in states where they have a trifecta will eventually get on board with the most aggressive gerrymandering possible for after 2026? Some redistricting analysts suggested that the math for Democrats controlling the House in 2028 once the Republican gerrymandered maps get fully implemented gets very difficult if we don’t target as many seats as possible with our own gerrymandering but I’m worried that some Democrats still aren’t there.

You have every right to be concerned. What has happened since the Supreme Court decision gutting Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act and greenlighting racial gerrymandering across the Deep South represents the greatest threat to democracy, minority representation in Congress, and Democratic chances this fall. Thirty percent of the Congressional Black Caucus could see their seats eliminated. The House will be more Republican and even more White. Decades of progress have been wiped away based on the whims of six justices.

Prior to the double whammy of the Supreme Court ruling and the Virginia court ruling striking down the new, voter-approved map, Democrats were on a glide path to winning the House by a relatively substantial margin. Now, that once-certain outcome is in some measure of doubt. By letting the GOP redraw the maps so close to the election, the Supreme Court is attempting to give Trump a measure of political immunity to go along with the legal immunity they granted him in 2024.

As I wrote last week, Democrats can still win the House. In fact, I think they remain favorites to do so. However, the magnitude of that victory will be less than it should be because of the new maps. Democrats will win fewer seats because the Supreme Court gave the GOP a bunch of seats.

But the battle does not end there. The Supreme Court has ushered in a new Hobbesian era of American politics that will require Democrats to fundamentally shift strategies going forward.

Here’s what we need to do.

First, recognize that we are now in a state of total political war. There is nothing Republicans won’t do to cling to power. Democrats must be willing to respond in kind. We cannot abide by archaic norms or the old way of doing things. We need to view everything through the prism of how we fight back against Republican efforts to rig our politics.

Last week, Virginia Democrats considered a bold plan to lower the age limit for Supreme Court justices so they could appoint an entirely new set of justices to approve the maps. They did not pursue that audacious plan because it was logistically impossible, but unfortunately, those sorts of ideas must be on the table going forward.

Second, we must put real pressure on elected officials in Democratically controlled states to redraw their maps in the most maximalist fashion possible. Many of them will be resistant, because of pressure from constituent groups and fears of angering swing or independent voters. Activists and organizers should protest, donors should withhold money, and content creators or others with platforms need to call them out aggressively. As we have already seen in 2026, Republicans will leave almost no stone unturned in the search for more political power. We have to be willing to repeal or find creative, legal workarounds for anti-gerrymandering laws and amendments. Anything short of a maximalist approach is unacceptable and akin to surrender.

Third, we are now in a state of perpetual redistricting. Every single cycle, Republicans and Democrats will look to redraw the maps in their favor. Control of Congress (and the nation’s legislative agenda) will be won and lost at the state level. We need to take a large portion of the hundreds of millions of dollars being spent largely on shitty TV ads in Congressional races and invest them in state legislative races. This includes a real, sustained, well-financed effort to build power in red states. The days of focusing all of our energy and money on six to ten swing districts are over. If you win a House seat in 2028 but lose the state legislature, that House seat will be gone by 2030.

Finally, if and when we have power again, we must use it relentlessly and somewhat ruthlessly to unrig the system and rebalance the scales — including a ban on partisan gerrymandering, national automatic voter registration, ending the filibuster, and Supreme Court reform.

Look, I hate gerrymandering. I wish we could put the genie back in the bottle. But the only way to stop this is to pass a national ban on partisan gerrymandering, and the only way to do that is for Democrats to control the White House, Senate, and House in 2029. That cannot happen if we unilaterally disarm.

Voters are favoring fresh candidates and traditional resume strengths (experience, time in Washington) are now revealed as electoral negatives. When did this shift take place and how long do you think our partisan model of electability has been out of alignment with the public’s?

Any discussion of electability is inherently fraught because it’s an impossible concept to prove. Any candidate who wins is by definition electable, and any who loses is unelectable. But I think you’re hitting on something important going on within the Democratic establishment. The people making the decisions, particularly in the Senate, tend to favor candidates who remind them of themselves — experienced politicians with a track record of winning. This is why Chuck Schumer and the DSCC supported Janet Mills, a 78-year-old incumbent governor, over Graham Platner, an oysterman, veteran, and harbor master who had never run for office.
